text: The patient is positioned in the Leo Davis position with a left tilt of fifteen degrees, allowing the small intestine to fall to the left side of the abdominal cavity. The assistant lifts the terminal ileum using intestinal forceps. The surgeon separates the terminal ileum from the posterior peritoneum, reaching the ileocecal peritoneum laterally and the superior mesenteric artery trunk medially. The assistant lifts the terminal ileum again as the surgeon enters the incision gap. The mesocolon of the right hemicolon is separated from the duodenum and pancreas head, both bluntly and sharply, up to the duodenal bulb. The patient is repositioned to a dorsal elevated position with a twenty-degree angle and a fifteen-degree left tilt to expose the gastric colon ligament. The assistant lifts the greater curvature, opens the gastrocolic ligament at the transverse colon's midpoint, and separates the hepatic flexure to the lateral peritoneum border. Gauze is placed at the anterior lobe of the transverse mesocolon as a marker for the next step. A horizontal incision is made at the root of the transverse mesocolon to guide the anatomical endpoint of the superior mesenteric vein. The assistant lifts the mesocolon to create a forty-five-degree angle with the posterior peritoneum, then incises the ileocolic mesenteric avascular zone below the ileocolic vessel. The mesoilium is incised along the superior mesenteric vein trunk to facilitate locating the superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon exposes the root of the ileocolic fascia from the right side of the superior mesenteric vein. The ileocolic vein is ligated and incised, followed by the ileocolic artery. The absence of the right colonic vein is noted. The superior mesenteric vein is dissociated towards the head side, and the root of the right colic artery is ligated and incised. The patient's right colonic vein is observed to originate from the gastrocolic vein trunk.

The surgeon ligates and incises the root of the middle colic artery and the accessory middle colic vein, followed by the middle colic vein, which flows into the superior mesenteric vein. The colonic branch of the gastrocolic vein trunk is also ligated and incised. The surgeon continues to dissociate the toes gap to the right until reaching the hepatic flexure, separating the adhesion of the hepatic flexure and retroperitoneum from the genital fascia surface. The assistant lifts the iliopsoas muscle, allowing the surgeon to sharply dissociate the lateral peritoneum around the paracolic sulcus of the ascending colon. The mesocolon of the right hemicolon is completely resected.

The patient is positioned in a dorsal elevated position with a twenty-degree angle and tilted to the right by fifteen degrees. The assistant lifts the gastric body while the surgeon dissociates the omentum medius levers, exposing the lower edge of the pancreas. The splenic flexure of the colon is completely dissociated along the pancreatic body and tail. The surgeon dissociates the left toes gap along the lower edge of the pancreas and ligates and incises the inferior mesenteric vein.

The assistant lifts the mesentery near the stump of the inferior mesenteric vein, while the surgeon incises the medial boundary of the left mesocolon upwards and leftwards along the right side of the inferior mesenteric vein. The toes gap is dissociated leftwards until reaching the lateral peritoneum, separating the descending colon from the lateral peritoneum. The patient is placed in the Leo Davis position and tilted to the right by fifteen degrees. The assistant lifts the sigmoid colon, and the surgeon dissociates the retroperitoneum from the right side of the sigmoid mesocolon. The surgeon then enters the left toes gap along the right side of the inferior mesenteric artery, exposing and ligating the root of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon completes the dissociation of the left toes gap, ensuring careful protection of the left ureter and autonomic nerve during the procedure.

The surgeon places gauze to protect the left ureter, gonad, and iliac vessels. The assistant pushes the sigmoid colon to the right, exposing its left mesentery. The surgeon incises the left white line of Toldt, separating the sigmoid and descending colon from the lateral peritoneum. The descending colon is dissociated upwards and towards the pelvic entrance downwards. The posterior rectal space is dissociated downward along the Toldt gap, continuing to the pelvic diaphragm. The anterior rectal fascia is separated from the posterior vaginal wall, ensuring the integrity of the posterior rectal mesangium. The rectum is completely dissociated by dividing the lateral rectal ligaments. The anus is resected via the perineum, and the specimen is removed completely before anal closure. Continuous suturing with absorbable barbed wire is performed to close the pelvic peritoneum after pneumoperitoneum reconstruction. An ileostomy is performed in the right lower quadrant.
